engineering.translation: Krishna names the structural location of kāma. It sits in three places: the senses (indriyāṇi), the mind (manas), and the intellect (buddhi). Through these three layers it deludes the dehī — the embodied actor — by covering jñāna. The three-layer diagnosis is doctrinal: the obstacle is not unitary; it operates through a specific layered architecture. In engineering this maps cleanly: kāma sits in input-streams (senses → notification streams, Slack pings, GitHub events, Datadog alerts), in attention (manas → the attentional-allocation layer that decides which input gets processed), and in commitment (buddhi → the layer that decides which engagement becomes work). The verse predicts that the actor's distraction is structured across these three layers and the remedy must address each layer in turn.
engineering.concrete_scenario: A staff engineer at a Postgres-shaped fintech catalogues her own afternoon distraction by layer. Layer one — senses (indriyāṇi): the Slack notification chime, the email-icon badge in Chrome, the Datadog alert on her phone, the GitHub @-mention on the second monitor. The input streams are the entry points. Layer two — mind (manas): even with the inputs muted, her attention drifts. The mind generates internal pings — 'I should check Slack,' 'I wonder if my PR has been reviewed,' 'I should look at the dashboard' — without external trigger. Layer three — intellect (buddhi): even with the mind held steady, the commitment layer wavers — the small voice that says 'this work is not as important as that other thing,' the framing that re-prioritises mid-task. The engineer realises that her usual focus-discipline addresses only the first layer (notification-blocking, full-screen mode, do-not-disturb). The second layer (the mind's internal pings) and the third layer (the buddhi's framing-shifts) operate underneath. Her mid-afternoon collapse always begins at one of those deeper layers. The verse predicted exactly this architecture.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if read as a flat list rather than a layered structure. The verse names a specific architecture — sense-input enters first, then mind allocates attention, then intellect commits — and the remedy follows the architecture. An engineer who reads the verse as 'distraction is in three places' rather than 'distraction is in three layered places' has misread it.
engineering.counter_example: Sometimes the distraction enters at the top layer and propagates down — the engineer who reframes their morning ('this work doesn't matter as much as I thought') has had buddhi-level disturbance, and the sense-level distractions are downstream. The verse's general direction (sense → mind → intellect) holds for the typical case; reverse-cascade cases require the prescription to be applied at the actual entry-layer.
engineering.implication: When auditing distraction, locate it by layer. Layer one (senses): can it be stopped at notification level? Layer two (mind): does it persist when notifications are off? Layer three (intellect): does it persist when the mind is steady? The verse's three-layer architecture is the map; the remedy follows.
engineering.quotable_line: Distraction operates in three layered places: input streams, attention, commitment. The verse names the architecture; the engineer's remedy must follow it.

iterations.mutation: v0 generation for BG 3.40: source pack triangulated; engineering layer anchored on chapter thesis and the kāma-as-enemy multi-verse argument (3.36-3.43).
